title: OOP
content:
Object-oriented Programming
Object-oriented programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm that organizes code around objects, which are self-contained units that bundle data and functions together. This approach uses concepts like classes (templates for objects), encapsulation (bundling data and methods), inheritance (reusing properties from other classes), and polymorphism (objects taking on many forms) to create modular, reusable, and maintainable code. An object oriented program is basically a collection of objects and relationships among them and they communicate or interact with each other using message passing. Four main concept of OOPs are abstraction, encapsulation, inheritance and polymorphism which help us to write modular, reusable, extensible and generic software system.
Classes and Objects
A class body contains contains specification of a set of fields which defines attributes of the objects and a set of methods which defines behaviours of the objects. Class is the unit of modularization in object oriented program. A class is a blueprint which defines the attributes (fields) and behaviours (methods) common to a number of objects. A class is a user defined blueprint based on which objects are created. Object is a concrete entity with a set of attributes and a set of behaviours. Object also have identify, lifetime and scope and belongs to a class. Fields defines the attributes of objects instantiated from a class. Methods defines the behaviors of objects instantiated from a class. Objects maintain state in instance variables (attributes). Objects are allocated from the heap and accessed by reference variables. Objects communicates with one another by sending and receiving message.
CodeDesc
class Snake { Size size; Color color; Point position; void move(Dir dir) {} void eat(Food food) {} } Defines attributes and behaviours of snakes. Behaviours are used to change states. State is combination of attributes values. Attributes = {size, color, position} Behaviours = {move, eat} Attributes = Form
Encapsulation
Encapsulation is the process of wrapping of related data and code into a single unit which modularizes our software system. Encapsulation also help us to achieve data hiding which is the process of hiding sensitive data of the objects from the outside world. Encapsulation refers to the bundling of data with the mechanisms or methods that operate on the data. Encapsulation also refer to the limiting of direct access to some of that data, such as an object's components. Essentially encapsulation prevents external code from being concerned with the internal workings of an object. Encapsulation is java is achieved through class. Encapsulation makes out software system modular ie. we can change one part of the code without affecting other parts of code. Data hiding is the process of hiding sensitive data of the object from the user of the object. Data hiding is a programming concept which protects the data from direct modification by other parts of the program. Data hiding is achieved by declaring fields as private and providing setter and getter methods for each field to access and update the value. Data hiding is closely related to abstraction and encapsulation. Data hiding is achieved through encapsulation. Advantages of encapsulation:
  • Data security: help us to make fields of a class read-only or write-only.
  • Encapsulation gives a clear and modular structure to our software system.
  • Program is well organized so easy to maintain.
Inheritance
Inheritance is a mechanism by which one class acquires the attributes (fields) and functionalities (methods) of another existing class. This mechanism promotes code reusability and establishes a hierarchical is-a relationship between classes. Reusability helps us to avoid duplicate code and data. Inheritance helps us to define relationship among classes. Inheritance enable us to create modules that does not need to be changed when new attributes or functionalities is added to the class. Kind of inheritance:
  • Single inheritance - when a class inherits another class.
  • Multi-level inheritance - when a class inherits derived class.
  • Hierarchical inheritance - when a class is inherited by many classes.
Abstraction
Abstraction is a mechanism of hiding unnecessary details and showing only essential details to the user by separating interface from its implementation. Abstraction also generalises by capturing the common essense among unrelated classes. Main goal of the abstraction it to reduce the complexity of the design and implementation process of software system. Abstraction can be achieved through interface and abstract class. Abstract method is a method that is declared without implementation. Abstract method must always be redefined in the subclass. Abstract method only have signature. Abstract method can not be final. Class which contains abstract method must be declared abstract. Abstract class is one of two ways to achieve abstraction in Java. Abstract class declared with abstract keyword. Abstract class cannot be instanciated. Abstract class cannot be final. Abstract class always have a default constructor. Abstract class can have parameterized constructors. Abstract class is created to be extended or inherited. Abstract class can have abstract and non-abstract methods. A class that extends an abstract class must implement all its abstract method. Interface is one of two ways to achieve abstraction in java. Interface is completely abstract class. Interface methods are by default abstract and public. Interface variables are by default public, static and final. Interface specifies only what needs to be done. Childrens will decide how to do in their own way. Like abstract class interface cannot be instanciated. Interface allows us to group several related classes as siblings. Method = Method header + method body. Method header is an abstraction of a block of code. Abstract method is independent of implementation which can have many implementations. Abstraction: Complete abstraction can be achieved through interface. Security: Hides implementation details and only show the important details of an object. Multiple inheritance: Can be achieved through interface because a class can implements multiple interfaces.
Interface
Cell
Polymorphism
Polymorphism refers to one-to-many relationship, which allows an entity such as an variable, a method or an object to have more than one form. Polymorphism enables us to deal with generalities and let the compiler and runtime environment to handle the specific. Polymorphism in Java can be achieved through method overloading, method overriding and Generics. Polymorphism is the provision of a single interface to entities of different types or the use of a single symbol to represent multiple different types. Field level polymorphism: a reference variable of class T can point to object of class E as well as object of subclass of E. Many methods can have one common name.
  • One method can accept many types of arguments.
  • One method header can be associated with many method body.
  • One type of reference variable can be used to access many type of objects.
Subtype-polymorphism
Subtype polymorphism is a form of type polymorphism where a variable of a superclass type refer to and object of a subclass type, allowing a single interface to represent different type of objects. Which means code written for a superclass can be used with instances of its subclasses and at runtime, the program calls the correct method based on the object's actual type. Inheritance and substitutability - a subclass is considered a subtype of its superclass. This relationship allows an object of the subclass to be substituted for an object of the superclass. Dynamic method selection - the specific method tha is executed is determined at runtime, not at compile time. Single interface for multiple types - this allows us to write code that operates on superclass type and have it work correctly with any of its subclasses.
Method-overloading
Process of writing multiple methods with the same name in the same class is called method overloading. Method-overloading is a kind of static polymorphism.
  • Method overloading increases the readability of the program.
  • Avoids the need to memorize multiple method names.
  • Method overloading makes our code more generic.
  • Method overloading support clean code.
  • The complexity of the code is reduced.
  • Compiler binds the method call with an approriate method body.
Method overloading is achieved by:
  • Changing the number of arguments.
  • Changing the data type of the arguments.
Method-overriding
Method-overriding is the process of redefining a parent class instance method into its child class is known as method overriding. An instance method defined in the parent class is redefined into its child class to give specific implementation of the parent class method. Referenced object type, not the reference variable type determines which method will be called. When we create a parent class reference variable and assign a child class object then calling an overridden method on that variable will call the child class behaviour.
  • Final methods can not be overridden.
  • Static methods can not be overridden.
  • Private methods can not be overridden.
  • Child class method is called overriding method.
  • Parent class method is called overridden method.
  • Overridden methods are dynamically dispatched.
  • Finalize method can be overridden to free system resources.
  • Overridden parent class method can be called using super keyword.
  • Runtime polymorphism can be achieved through method overriding.
  • Abstract methods can only be overridden by the first concrete class.
  • The access level cannot be more restrictive than the overridden method.
  • Override annotation denotes that the method overrides the base class method.
Later Binding
Dynamic binding is a process in which a call to overridden method is resolved at the runtime rather than compile time. Which means that the code to be executed for a specific method call is not known until runtime. Dynamic dispatch is used when multiple classes contain different implementations of the same method which provides a mechanism for selecting the function to be executed from various function alternatives at the runtime. Dynamic binding (dispatch) is the process of linking function call to a specific sequence of code (function) at run-time.
Association
Association represents a relationship where one object is linked to another object. Association describes relationships between classes. The association can be one-way or two-way. Which implies that two classes are related, but neither class owns the other. Associations relate entities that can exist independently of each other - such as authors and books.
Aggregation
Aggregation is a special form of association where one object contains another object. However, the contained object can exist independently of the container. Lifetime of the contained object is not dependent on the lifetime of the container. Aggregation represents has-a relationship between objects. Composition represents part-of relationship between objects. An aggregation is a collection or the gathering of things together. Aggregation - A has an instance of B, B can exists without A. Composition - A has an instance of B, B cannot exists without A.
Composition
Composition is a stronger form of association where one object contains another object and the contained object cannot exist independently of the container. The lifetime of the contained object depends on the lifetime of the container. In composition if owning object is garbage collected the owned object will also be which is not the case in aggregation. Composition means the child entity (order items) is part of a whole and cannot exist independently of the parent entity (order). The child entity is contained in the parent entity and can only be accessed via the parent entity. Both composition and aggregation express a has-a relationship. Composition expresses a strong has-a relationship (whole-part ownership); aggregation expresses a weak has-a relationship (association without ownership).
Composition vs Inheritance
Both composition and inheritance used to solve the same problem that is code reuse. Process of building complex objects from simpler ones is called object composition. Use inheritance only when you want both hierarchical reuse of code and subtype polymorphism.
Dependency Injection
Dependency injection generally means passing a dependent object as a parameter to a method rather than having the method create the dependent object. Dependency injection is a software design pattern that allow us to develop loosely-coupled software. Dependecy injection allows us to depends more on abstraction rather than the concrete implementation, which promotes loosely-coupled architecture, flexibility and pluggability within our code. What it means in practice is that the method does not have a direct dependency on a particular implementation, any implementation that meets the requirements can be passed as a parameter. Dependency injection is a programming technique where an object or function receives its dependencies (other objects or functions it needs) from an external source, rather than creating them itself. This approach, which is a form of Inversion of Control, makes code more flexible, maintainable, and testable by decoupling components and allowing them to be replaced or mocked easily.

title: SOLID
content:
SOLID
Solid principle is a set of guidelines for writing code that makes it easier to manage, extend and debug. Solid principle help us break big problem into smaller easier to handle parts. Solid reduces dependencies on code so changes in one area won't break another. Solid reduces tight coupling, making code more flexible and easier to maintain.
Single Responsibility Principle
Cell
Open-closed Principle
Cell
Linksov-substitution Principle
Cell
Interface-segregation Principle
Cell
Dependency-inversion Principle
Cell

title: DP
content:
Design Patterns
Design patterns are reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design. Design patterns are solutions or techniques to avoid code design problem. Design patterns help us write robust, clean, modular and extensible code. They provide a template for solving design issues in various contexts, helping developers write code that is more maintainable, scalable and understandable. Design patterns are divided into three categories:
  • Creational patterns - Deals with object creation.
  • Structural patterns - Deals with object composition.
  • Behavioral Patterns - Deals with object interaction and responsibility.
Reusability - provide a reusable solution to common software design problems which saves developers from reinventing the wheel whenever they encounter a new problem. Instead, they can use a tried and tested pattern to solve the problem.
Maintainability - promote clean, organized and maintainable code which makes it easier for developers to maintain and modify the code as the software evolves.
Rapid-developement - provide a standard way of solving common software design problems which saves time and effort in the desing and development process, allowing developers to focus on other aspects of the software.

Creational: Singleton, Builder, Factory Method, Abstract Factory and Prototype.
Structural: Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight and Proxy. Behavioral: CoR, Command, Iterator, Mediator, Memento, Observer, State, Strategy, Template and Visitor.
Adapter
Adapter pattern is a structural design pattern that allows objects with incompatible interfaces to work together. Adapter acts as a bridge between two incompatible interfaces. Adapter pattern converts the interface of a class into another interface that the client expects. Adapter lets classes work together that couldn’t otherwise because of incompatible interfaces. Adapter catches calls for one object and transforms them to format and interface recognizable by the second object. Adapter pattern should be used when:
  • There is an existing class and its interface does not match the one you need.
  • We need to connect systems or components that were not built to work together.
Components:
  • Target: The interface the client expects.
  • Adaptee: The existing class that needs adapting.
  • Adapter: The class that implements the target interface and internally uses or adapts the adaptee.
Pros and Cons:
  • Reusability of existing functionality.
  • Keeps existing code untouched.
  • Code can become complex if many adapters are introduced.
  • Sometimes it's better to refactor than use adapters excessively.
Facade
Facade pattern is a structural design pattern that provides a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. Facade defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use. A complex interface to the client is considered as a fault in the design of a system. Facade pattern makes a complex interface easier to use using a Facade class. When-to use:
  • When you want to simplify the usage of a complex system.
  • When there are multiple subsystems that need to be integrated.
  • To decouple clients from the internal components of a system.
  • When you want to create a layered architecture.
Advantages:
  • Simplifies complex interfaces.
  • Makes the system easier to use.
  • Promotes loose coupling.
  • Improves code readability.
Composite
Composite pattern is a kind of structural design pattern used when we want to treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly. Composite pattern is especially useful when you deal with hierarchical tree structures, such as file systems, DOM tree or UI components. Composite lets clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly. When-to use: when we want to compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Components:
  • Component: interface or abstract class for all objects in the hierarchy, both composite and leaf.
  • Leaf: represents individual objects (end nodes) which implements the base Component.
  • Composite: a container that holds child Components.
  • Client: uses the Component interface to interact with objects in the hierarchy uniformly.
Use cases:
  • GUI frameworks (Swing or HTML DOM trees)
  • Organization hierarchy (employees, managers)
  • File system (files and directories)
  • Document structures (paragraphs, sections, characters)
Proxy
Proxy pattern is a structural design pattern that provides a proxy for other objects to control access to them. Proxy implements the same interface as the real object and can add behavior like access control, logging, caching, lazy loading, etc. A proxy functions as an interface to something else. A proxy is a wrapper or agent object that the client calls to access the real serving object behind the scenes. Extra functionalities can be provided into proxy such as caching the operations on the actual object or checking pre-conditions before calling operations on the actual object. When-to use: when we need to perform additional operations (lazy loading, caching, access control) when accessing an object. Components:
  • Subject (Interface) – Declares common interface for RealSubject and Proxy.
  • RealSubject – The actual object that does the work.
  • Proxy – Controls access to RealSubject, implements the same interface.
Real-world example:
  • Java RMI – Uses proxy to call remote objects.
  • Spring AOP – Uses proxies for method interception.
  • Hibernate – Lazy-loaded entities are proxies.
  • Security managers – Proxy access to critical resources.
  • Credit card - Acts as a proxy for our bank account.
Flyweight
Flyweight pattern is a structural design pattern that reduces memory uses by sharing similar objects. Flyweight pattern uses a factory to manage these shared objects and ensures that each object is created only once. When we need large number of similar objects then creating and storing each object separately can take too much memory. Components:
  • Flyweight interface – Declares methods that accept extrinsic state.
  • ConcreteFlyweight – Implements Flyweight and stores intrinsic state.
  • FlyweightFactory – Manages a pool of flyweight objects.
  • Client – Maintains or computes extrinsic state and passes it to the flyweight.
Pros and Cons:
  • Reduces memory usage by sharing objects.
  • Increases performance when creating many similar objects.
  • Makes code more complex.
  • Requires careful separation of intrinsic and extrinsic state.
Decorator
Decorator is a structural design pattern that allows us to add new behavior or functionality to objects dynamically without altering their structure or modifying their source code. This is achieved by creating decorator classes that wrap the original object and add new behaviours or attributes. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality. Decorator design pattern allows behaviours to be added to individual objects, either statically or dynamically without affecting the behaviour of other objects from the same class. When-to use:
  • When we want to add responsibilities to individual objects, not an entire class.
  • When we want to keep classes open for extension but closed for modification.
Components:
  • Component: The interface or abstract class defining the operations.
  • Concrete component: The original object that we want to extend.
  • Decorator: Implements the same interface and has a reference to a Component object.
  • Concrete Decorators: Add new behaviors before or after delegating the request to the wrapped component.
Advantages:
  • Adheres to SRP and OCP.
  • Greater flexibility than static inheritance.
  • Can add or remove responsibilities at runtime.
Real-world examples:
  • Java IO Streams.
  • Spring's Transactional, Cacheable can be seen as decorators.
  • Spring Framework - Middleware or Interceptors decorate request and response handling.
Observer
Observer pattern is a behavioral design pattern that defines a one-to-many dependency between objects. In this pattern an object known as subject maintains a list of its dependent objects known as observers. When the subject's state changes, subject automatically notifies all its observers. The observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where, when one object's state changes, all objects that depend on it are notified and updated automatically. When-to use:
  • Use Observer when you want to automatically notify multiple objects of state changes.
  • Great for event-driven systems, GUIs, distributed messaging, and real-time updates.
  • Think of it as a push-based notification system for objects.
Components:
  • Subject – The core object being observed. Maintains a list of observers.
  • Observer – Interface that defines the update method.
  • ConcreteSubject – Implements subject and notifies observers.
  • ConcreteObserver – Implements observer and responds to state changes.
Visitor
Visitor Pattern is a behavioral design pattern that lets us add new operations to existing object structures without modifying their classes. This pattern helps separate algorithms from the objects on which they operate. Visitor pattern is useful in scenario when an object structure is stable but we frequently need to extend its behaviours with new operations. This pattern aligns with open-closed concepts. Components:
  • Visitor – Interface or abstract class for visiting elements.
  • ConcreteVisitor – Implements operations on concrete elements.
  • Element – Interface that defines accept(visitor)
  • ConcreteElement – Implements accept(visitor)
  • Client – Traverses object structure and applies visitor.
Real-world examples:
  • Filesystem utilities – Backup, search, indexing tools can visit folders and files.
  • Game engines – Collision detection or rendering can be implemented as visitors over game entities.
  • AST Traversal– Different visitors (semantic analyzer, code generator) operate on the same tree nodes.
  • Document converters – HTML, PDF, MD converters can visit document elements (paragraphs, images, links).
Pros and Cons:
  • Add new operations without changing existing object structures.
  • Encourages separation of concerns (data vs behavior).
  • Makes operations extensible and easy to manage.
  • Adding a new element (type) requires updating all visitors.
  • Involves double dispatch – one call from client to element, one from element to visitor.
Mediator
Mediator design pattern is a behavioral design pattern that promotes loose coupling between objects by centralizing communication between them. Instead of objects referring to each other directly, they communicate through a mediator object. This makes the system easier to maintain, modify, and scale, especially as the number of objects increases. Mediator pattern focuses on simplifying communication between objects by managing and coordinating their interations through a mediator. Mediator pattern is used for coordinating communication while the proxy pattern is used for controlling access. Components:
  • Mediator interface: Declares the communication contract used by components.
  • ConcreteMediator: Implements coordination logic and knows all components (colleagues).
  • Colleague: Individual objects that communicate via mediator. Knows about the mediator but not other colleagues.
Pros and Cons:
  • Promotes low coupling between components.
  • Simplifies maintenance and enhances reusability.
  • Centralizes complex communications.
  • Mediator can become a God object if it gets too complex.
  • Too much logic inside the mediator can lead to poor scalability.
Iterator
Iterator pattern is a behavioral design pattern that provides a way to access the elements of a collection (like a list or tree) sequentially without exposing its underlying representation. Iterator pattern is used to traverse elements in a container. Iterator pattern decouples container traversal algorithms from containers. Intent is to provide a standard way to traverse through a collection of objects without exposing its internal structure. Components:
  • Iterator (Interface) – declares the interface for accessing elements.
  • ConcreteIterator – implements the Iterator interface and keeps track of the current position.
  • Aggregate (Interface) – declares a method to return an iterator.
  • ConcreteAggregate – implements the method to return an iterator instance.
Advantages:
  • Separates collection traversal logic from collection data structure.
  • Uniform traversal for various types of collections.
  • Supports multiple iterations simultaneously.
Chain-of-Responsibility
CoR design pattern is a behavioral design pattern that decouples sender and receiver of a request by connecting multiple handlers in a chain. The request is then passed along the chain until it is processed by one of the handlers. When-to use: when we have multiple objects that can handle a request but the specific object that handles it is determined at runtime. Components:
  • Handler interface: Declares a method for handling requests and a reference to the next handler.
  • ConcreteHandler: Handles requests it is responsible for or forwards them to the next handler.
  • Client: Sends the request to the first handler in the chain.
Workflow:
  • A client sends a request to the first handler in the chain, if the handler can handle it, it does.
  • Otherwise, it forwards the request to the next handler in the chain.
  • This continues until the request is handled or the end of the chain is reached.
Command
Command design pattern is a behavioral design pattern that turns a request into a stand-alone object containing all the information about the request. This object can then be passed, stored, queued, and executed at a later time. Command pattern: an object is used to encapsulate all the information needed to perform an action or trigger an event at a later time, this information includes method name, the object that owns the method and the parameter for the method. Command pattern is perfect for features like scheduling, logging, undo and redo operations. Components:
  • Command (Interface) – declares an interface for executing an operation.
  • ConcreteCommand – defines a binding between a receiver and an action.
  • Receiver – knows how to perform the operation associated with the command.
  • Invoker – asks the command to carry out the request.
  • Client – creates the command object and sets its receiver.
Pros and Cons:
  • Decouples objects invoking operations from those performing them.
  • Easy to add new commands without changing existing code.
  • Supports undo-redo, logging, and macro recording.
  • May result in lots of small classes ex. one per command.
Strategy
Strategy pattern allows us to define a set of algorithms and encapsulate them into independent classes so that they can be used interchangeably. Strategy pattern is a behavioral design pattern that separates the definition of algorithms from their usage, allowing the algorithms to vary independently of the client code. Strategy pattern is all about enabling flexibility in behavior by defining a family of algorithms, encapsulating each one, and making them interchangeable. Instead of hardcoding a behavior into a class, you delegate it to an object that implements a common interface. Core idea of this design pattern is to define a family of interchangeable algorithms. When to use this pattern:
  • When have multiple algorithms (or behaviors) for a specific task.
  • When want to switch algorithms dynamically at runtime.
  • When want to avoid cluttering your code with long conditionals (like if-else or switch-case statements).
Components:
  • Strategy Interface: Common interface for all supported behaviors.
  • Concrete Strategies: Different implementations of the strategy.
  • Context: Uses a Strategy object and delegates it to the strategy interface.
Advantages:
  • Open-Closed Principle: Add new strategies without modifying existing code.
  • Cleaner Code: Eliminates repetitive conditionals.
  • Interchangeability: Strategies can be swapped out easily.
Memento
Memento design pattern is a behavioral design pattern that allows us to capture and externalize an object's internal state so that it can be restored later, without violating encapsulation. Memento pattern helps store the state of an object and restores when needed. Commonly used to implement to undo-redo functionality. We can use memento design pattern when we need to capture and restore the state of an object without violating encapsulation. Command design pattern encapsulates actions, Memento encapsulates state. Components:
  • Originator - The object whose state we want to save and restore.
  • Memento - A object that acts as a snapshot of Originator’s state. It is immutable and doesn’t expose internal details.
  • Caretaker - Object that knows when to save and restore Originator’s state but doesn't know the content of Memento.
Real-world examples:
  • TextEditors - Undo-redo functionality.
  • Games - Save and restore player progress.
  • Graphics Design - Photoshop history panel.
  • Databases - Rollback transactions.
  • Financial Apps - Restoring previous transaction data for audit.
Comparisons:
  • Command - Encapsulates actions, Memento encapsulates state.
  • State - Allows dynamic change of state, Memento stores past state.
  • Prototype - Clones the whole object while Memento saves only necessary internal state.
Drawbacks:
  • Makes rollback logic external and sometimes complex.
  • Requires proper management of mementos: make Memento immutable.
  • Can consume a lot of memory if too many states are stored: Limit how many mementos are stored (undo levels).

title: Thread
content:
Multithreading
Multithreading is a process of executing multiple threads simultaneously. Multithreaded programs are capable of doing more than one tasks in parallel. Multithreading allows different parts of the program to execute simultaneously. Applications that perform several tasks that are not dependent on one another benefit the most from multithreading. Parallelism is about doing multiple tasks at once. Concurrency is about dealing with multiple tasks at once. Multi-threaded program: multiple instructions are executed in parallel or atleast appear to be executed.
Thread
A thread of execution is the smallest sequence of programmed instructions that can be managed independently by a OS scheduler. Threads of a process share its executable code and the values of its dynamically allocated variables and non-thread-local global variables at any given time. Local primitive variables are thread-safe because each thread has separate stack memory. Local reference variables are not thread-safe because each thread share heap memory. A thread is a sequence of instructions within a program that can be executed independently of other code. Thread is a dispatchable unit of work, includes a processor context (program counter and stack pointer) and its own data area for a stack. Each thread is executing the same program code and shares the same data area and heap however each thread has it own stack containing local variables. Eahc thread has a priority, thread scheduler uses priority to determine which thread should be allowed to execute. Threads with higher priority are executed in preference to threads with lower priority. When multiple threads are ready to execute then JVM will selects and executes the Runnable thread which has the highest priority. In case two threads have the same priority then JVM will execute them in FIFO order.
Thread State
New: A newly created thread that has not yet started the execution. Runnable: Either running or ready for execution but waiting for resource allocation. Blocked: Thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock to enter (execute) a synchronized-block or synchronized-method. Waiting: Thread is waiting for some other thread to perform a particular action without any time limit. Timed-waiting: Thread is waiting for some other thread to perform a specific action for a specified period. Terminated: Thread has completed its execution. A thread goes to waiting state once it calls wait on an object or Thread.join method. A thread in the waiting state is waiting for a signal from another thread which will then remain in this state until another thread calls notify or notifyAll method. Once this thread is notified, it will not be runnable as it might be that other threads are also notified using notifyAll, so it will be in blocked state till it gets its chance. A blocking state will occur whenever a thread tries to acquire lock on object and some other thread is already holding the lock. In the blocking state, a thread is about to enter a synchronized section but there is another thread currently running inside a synchronized block on the same object. The first thread must then wait for the second thread to exit block.
Shared Data
When multiple threads try to modify a shared resource at the same time then the final outcome is non deterministic. We must explicitly synchronize concurrent access to a shared resource in a multi-threaded environment. When a thread enters synchronized code (normally by calling the synchronized method of that object) it takes the lock on that object and thus other threads cannot enters synchronized code because there is only one lock per object. Cached shared data causes a thread not to notice that data has been changed by the another thread. Suppose you are buying theatre tickets online and after you pick your seats and are entering your credit card information, someone else buys that seat and they are no longer available. Suppose multiple threads have access to a collection object and they are adding and removing at same time then there will be an error unless the collection is thread-safe.
Race Condition
Race condition occurs when multiple threads try to update mutable shared data at the same time. Two threads simultaneously reading data from the same object is not a problem but if both threads are simultaneously try to update the same object or if one thread is updating the object while the other is reading it then memory inconsistencies may arise.
  • Read-read sharing is safe.
  • Both read-write and write-write races are a problem.
Deadlock
A deadlock occurs when there is more than one thread, each waiting for a resource held by another which occurs when multiple threads acquires the same set of lock in different order. The most obvious kind of resource is an object monitor but any resource that causes blocking such as wait-notify can qualify. Deadlock happens when two or more threads become interlocked:
  • Thread A holds the lock in object1 but is waiting on object2
  • Thread B holds the lock in object2 but is waiting on object1
Thread Synchronization
Thread synchronization is a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent processes or threads do not simultaneously execute some particular program segment known as critical section. Processes' access to critical section is controlled by using synchronization techniques. When one thread starts executing the critical section (serialized segment of the program) the other thread should wait until the first thread finishes. There is no any guarantee about exactly when a blocked thread is unblocked when the synchronized block is free again. This is typically up to the OS or execution platform to coordinate the unblocking of blocked threads. Thread synchronization is a mechanism which ensures that two or more concurrent threads do not execute some particular section of program especially critical section. There are two types of thread synchronization mutual exclusion and inter-thread communication. Following are some classic problems of synchronization:
  • Producer-consumer problem
  • Readers-writers problem
  • Dining philosopher problem
Thread Cooperation
Thread cooperation is achieved using the wait-set which is associated with the monitor and wait-notify mechanism. Cooperation is important when one thread needs some data to be in a particular state and another thread is responsible for getting the data into that state. When a thread invokes the wait method respect to an object then the thread will be suspended and added to the wait-set to wait until some other thread invokes notify or notifyAll on the same object. If a thread was put in the queue by a call to wait then it must be unfrozen by a call to notify before it can be scheduled for execution again. Notify method is used for waking up threads that are in the wait set of the monitor of a particular object. A thread may call wait, notify or notifyAll on an object only if it owns the monitor lock of that object.
Mutual Exclusion
Mutual exclusion or atomicity ensures that write functionality is thread safe. Mutual exclusion is a property of concurrency control, which is instituted for the purpose of preventing race conditions. Race condition occures when a shared resource is accessed by different actors at the same time. Both mutex and semaphore helps managing access to shared resources but they do it in different ways. Mutex is suited for exclusive access whereas semaphore is suited for limited concurrency. Mutual exclusion helps keep threads from interfering with each other while sharing data. Mutual exclusion ensures that only one thread can have access to shared data at a time. Mutual exclusion ensures that write functionality is thread-safe. Mutex is a program object which prevents simultaneous access to a shared data by multiple threads. Critical section is region of the code where shared data between multiple threads is accessed. Shared data is considered as a critical area. Mutual exclusion can be achieved through:
  • Synchronized block
  • Synchronized method
  • Static synchronization
Monitor
Synchronization is built around an internal entity known as the monitor. Monitors are used to achieved mutual exclusion and threads cooperation. A monitor is an entity that possesses a lock, a wait-queue and an entry-queue. Every Java objects has a monitor associated with them. Lock enforces mutual exclusive access to synchronized code block or method invoked on the associated object. Wait-set is the set of all the threads waiting on an monitor object that is used to provide coordination between multiple threads. Entry-set is the set of all the threads who has already requested for the lock and the lock is not acquired by them yet.
Locks
A lock is a tool for controlling access to a shared resource by multiple threads. Every objects in java has an intrinsic lock associated with it. Only one thread at a time can acquire the lock and all access to the shared resource requires that the lock be acquired first. When a new thread tries to acquire the lock and if already a thread owns the lock then that thread will be waiting on the entry set to acquire the lock. When the thread which is acquired the lock completes execution of its critical section then it will release the lock. Next thread will acquire the lock but this next thread is taken from the entry set and will be determined by JVM based on some criteria like FIFO.
Thread Pool
A thread pool is a collection of pre-created worker threads that are kept ready to perform tasks, avoiding the overhead of creating and destroying new threads for each task. When a task is submitted, it is assigned to an available thread from the pool. If all threads are busy, the task waits in a queue until a thread becomes free. Thread pools improve performance by reusing threads and help manage resources by limiting the number of threads running concurrently. Key characteristics:
  • Worker threads: A set of threads that are created at the start and are always ready to execute tasks.
  • Task queue: A queue to hold tasks when all threads in the pool are busy.
  • Configurable: Thread pools allow configuration of the no. of threads, type of queue to use and how to handle rejected tasks.
How it works:
  • Task submission: Tasks are submitted to the thread pool, not directly to individual threads.
  • Task assignment: An available worker thread from the pool is assigned to the task.
  • Queueing: If all threads are busy, the task is placed in a waiting queue.
  • Thread reuse: After completing a task, the thread returns to the pool to wait for another task, instead of being terminated.
Advantages:
  • Improved performance: Reduces the significant cost of creating and destroying threads for each task.
  • Resource management: Limits the total no. of threads, preventing the system from running out of resources like memory.
  • Simplified programming: Developers can focus on the task logic rather than the complexities of managing thread lifecycles.
Reactive Programming
Reactive programming is a declarative programming paradigm concerned with data streams and the propagation of change. Reactive programming allows applications to react to updates automatically and efficiently. Like reacting to something when user clicks this button run this function or when file IO operation is complete run this function. Imperative means when we are doing all the steps, while declarative is where we are declaring this is what i want to happen and something is going to make that happen. An imperative approach involves explicitly writing out the sequence of steps to achieve a result, while a declarative approach describes the desired outcome and the programming language figures out the execution. Main purpose of reactive programming is to avoid blocking in our code. Synchronous = Sequential Asynchronous = Non-sequential List<Person> findByName(String name) -> void findByName(String name, Callback<List<Person>> persons BigDecimal getIncome(String name) -> void getIncome(String name, Callback<BigDecimal> income)
synchronized
A block of code marked with synchronized becomes a synchronized block. Keyword synchronized enforces exclusive access to the block of code. Only one thread is allowed to enter this block of code at the same time. Thread that first enters the synchronized block remains in runnable state. Synchronized blocks and synchronized methods are mutually exclusive. Synchronized method can be static method or instance method. The synchronized keyword does not support fairness. Fairness property provides a lock to the longest waiting thread (contention). Any thread can acquire lock once released, no preference can be specified. All other threads accessing the same block of code enters the blocked state. When a runnable thread exits the synchronized block then lock is released. Another thread is now allowed to enter the runnable state and place a new lock. When two or more threads modify an object then declare the methods that carry out the modification as synchronized.When a class has one or more synchronized methods, each object of the class gets a queue that holds all threads waiting to execute one of the synchronized methods. When a thread wants to execute a synchronized method on a given object, first it has to acquire the lock of that object. Once thread acquired the lock then it is allowed to execute any synchronized method on that object. When a thread calls a synchronized method on an object then the JVM ckecks the monitor lock for that object. If the monitor lock is unowned then ownership is assigned to the calling thread which is then allowed to proceed with the method call. If the monitor lock is owned by another thread then the calling thread will be put on hold until the monitor lock becomes available. When a thread exits a synchronized method it releases the monitor lock, allowing a waiting thread (if any) to proceed with its synchronized method call.

title: CS
content:
Hashing
Hashing is the process of converting an input of any size into a fixed-size string of characters using a hash function. A hash function is a mathematical process that converts a variable-length input into a fixed-length output called a hash or digest. These functions are one-way and collision-resistant, meaning they are easy to compute one way but very difficult to reverse, and different inputs should produce different outputs. Hash function must be also deterministic. Hashing is widely used for data integrity checks, secure password storage, and authentication in applications like cryptography, blockchain, and data structures.
Consistent Hashing
Consistent hashing is a hashing technique that minimizes the number of keys that need to be remapped when a hash table is resized, useful in distributed systems. Consistent hashing is a distributed hashing algorithm designed to address the problem of data redistribution when nodes are added or removed. With traditional hashing algorithms adding or removing node often result in alot of data needing to be distributed which can lead to system instability and performance degradation. Goal of consistent hashing is to minimize the need for rehashing when the number of nodes in a system changes. Consistent hashing is used in distributed systems, such as web caches, CDNs and DBs to efficiently distribute data or load across a variable number of servers or nodes.

title: ARCH
content:
Software Architecture
Architecture of a system describes its major components, its relationships and how they interact with each other. Software architecture is the high-level, strategic blueprint of a system, defining its overall structure, core components, and their interactions to meet business needs, while design is the tactical, low-level process of detailing how individual components will be built and work together. Think of architecture as defining the what and why (using a microservices pattern for scalability), whereas design is the how of specific implementation, like choosing a particular design pattern (Strategy pattern) to solve a problem within one of those services. Key decisions:
  • Choosing an overall pattern like microservices or serverless.
  • Addressing non-functional requirements like security, performance, and scalability across the system.
  • Defining the interactions between major components and systems.
Monolith
Monolithic architecture is a software design pattern where all components of an application like the user interface, business logic and data access layer are built and deployed as a single, unified unit. Disadvantages:
  • Tight coupling - modules are inter-dependent meaning changes in one module result in breaking of other module.
  • Failure in one module can brings down the entire application.
  • Any change requires re-deploying the whole system, even for small updates.
Client-server Model
The client-server model is a computing model where client devices request resources or services from a centralized server, which then processes the request and provides a response. Client-server model also called request-response model in which client sends a request and has to wait for the server's response before sending another one. Client-server model does not support continuos flow (live streaming) of data. For update client has to ask the server, server itself does not provides realtime updates.
Microservices
Microservice architecture is an architectural pattern that organizes an application into a collection of small, independent and loosely coupled services. Each service focuses on a specific business capability, can be developed, deployed and scaled independently and communicates with others through lightweight protocols like APIs. Main characteristics of microservice architecture is the ability to develop, deploy, operate and scale each module independently. These services are loosely coupled, independently deployable and often developed and scaled separately, enabling greater flexibility and agility in managing complex systems. Each component service in a microservices architecture can be developed, deployed, operated and scaled without affecting the functioning of other services. Services do not need to share any of their code or implementation with other services. Any communication between individual components happens via well-defined APIs. Advantages:
  • Agility: Faster delivery cycles, as teams can work and deploy services independently.
  • Scalability: The ability to scale individual services as needed is more efficient than scaling a monolith.
  • Resilience: Failure in one service is isolated, improving overall application reliability.
  • Team autonomy: Small teams can own and manage specific services, which promotes greater agility.
Challenges:
  • Complexity: Managing a distributed system can be more complex than a single monolithic application.
  • Communication overhead: Network latency and handling errors in service-to-service communication can be challenging.
  • Data consistency: Ensuring data consistency across multiple, independent services can be difficult.
Event-driven or EDA
Event-driven architecture is a software design pattern that allows system to detect, process and respond to events. A system design paradigm where components react to events rather than relying on synchronous request-response patterns

title: DB
content:
Database
Data is the digital representation of the information that can be stored on the electronic devices and transmitted over network. Date can be in form of numbers, text, images, videos, sounds etc. A database is a collection of data modeled and organized in a specific way. Software that manages databases are called database management system (DBMS). Depending on how data is modeled and organized, database is grouped into two categories (SQL, NoSQL). Data (Relational DB) = Structured into set of tables and set of relationships between tables.
Primary Key
A primary key is a column or set of columns in a database table that uniquely identifies each row. Uniqueness: Each value in the primary key column(s) must be unique, so no two rows have the same primary key. Non-null: A primary key column cannot have a null value because a null value cannot be used to uniquely identify a row. Automatic indexing: Most database systems automatically create a unique index on the primary key to speed up data retrieval. Single or composite: Can be a single column or a combn of multiple cols (composite key) that together, uniquely identify each row.
Foreign Key
A foreign key is a column in one table, known as the child table that references the primary key (or a unique key) of another table. A foreign key constraint is a rule in relational databases that establishes and enforces a link between two tables. A foreign key ensures referential integrity, meaning that relationships between tables remain consistent and valid. The primary purpose of a foreign key is to maintain referential integrity. This means that every value in the foreign key column (s) of the child table must either exist as a value in the referenced primary key column of the parent table, or be NULL (if the foreign key column is nullable). This prevents actions that would create orphan records in the child table, where a record refers to a non-existent record in the parent table. Foreign key is what makes relational database relational.
Indexing
Database indexing is a technique that improves the speed of data retrieval by creating a data structure, like a lookup table, that points to the data's location. When you create an index on a table column, a separate data structure is built with the values from that column and pointers to the corresponding rows in the main table. A clustered index physically sorts and stores a table's data based on its key values, like a dictionary, while a non-clustered index is a separate structure that contains the index keys and pointers to the data rows. This means a table can have only one clustered index but can have multiple non-clustered indexes. Clustered indexes are faster for data retrieval because the data is stored in the same order as the index, but they can increase overhead for data modification operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE). Non-clustered indexes are slower to query but more flexible, as they don't affect the physical order of the table data.
Sharding
Sharding is the process of dividing a database table (dataset) into smaller, more manageable pieces called shards, which are distributed across multiple machines or nodes. Sharding usually refers to splitting one large table’s data horizontally (row-wise) across multiple databases or servers. Each shard contains a subset of rows of the table. All shards have the same schema, just different data. Splits large datasets into smaller chunks (shards) to spread the load across servers.
ORM
Object-relational mapping (ORM) is the process of mapping java objects to relational tables of a relational database. Each row in the table correspond to the single object and each column in the table correspond to the each field of the object. ORM is an layer between object-oriented language and SQL language of relational database. ORM layer work like a adapter layer as it adapts the language of object graphs to the language of relational tables. Data is structured into object graphs in the object oriented program and relational tables in the relational database. JPA is the java standard API for object-relational mapping which is one of the many possible approach to ORM. API is a set of concepts that defines which objects within the application should be persisted and how it should persist them. JPA is only an specification and that it needs an implementation to work. Most popular impl of JPA is Hibernate. JPA is concerned with persistence, which means any mechanism by which java objects outlive the application process that created them. Advantages of using ORM: 1. Database independence.
2. Performance enhancements through caching.
3. Simplified development by abstracting SQL and automating table creation.
Connection Pool
Database connection pooling is a way to reduce the cost of opening and closing connections by maintaining a pool of open connections that can be passed from one database operation to another database operation as needed. This way, we are spared the expense of having to open and close a brand new connection for each operation the database is asked to perform. Connection pooling improves the performance of the application by reducing the latency associated with establishing new database connections, leading to faster query execution and overall application response times. A connection pooler is responsible for opening, closing and maintaining connections to the database on behalf of clients. For a client application to open a connection to a database, a surprising number of steps must occur:
  • Any DNS lookups required to locate the IP address of the database server
  • Conduct the three-way handshake required to establish a TCP connection to the server
  • Negotiate and enable encryption for the connection through a TLS handshake
  • Exchange preferences and requirements with the database software to establish the session parameters
  • Perform database authentication checks to establish the client's identity
  • Perform initial authorization checks to establish that the client has access to the requested database objects
  • Perform the actual query and return the results
  • Tear down the database session, TLS encryption, and TCP connection
ACID
ACID properties define the reliability and integrity of database transactions. They ensure that transactions are processed reliably and that the database remains in a consistent state, even in the face of system failures. Transactions are treated as a single, indivisible unit of work. Either all operations within a transaction are completed successfully, or none of them are. If any part of the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back to preventing partial updates. A transaction must bring the database from one valid state to another. This means that any transaction must respect all defined constraints, rules and restrictions of the database. Concurrent transactions should not interfere with each other. Each transaction should be isolated from others, as if they were running sequentially. This prevents issues like dirty reads (reading uncommitted data) or lost updates. Once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent and survive system failures. The database must guarantee that the data will be written to persistent storage and will not be lost due to power outages or other crashes.

title: DS
content:
Distributed System
Distributed computing involves multiple computers working together over a network to solve a problem, acting as a single, powerful system. It's a divide-and-conquer approach where tasks are broken down and processed concurrently on different nodes (computers), then the results are combined. This allows for greater scalability, performance, and resilience compared to using a single, centralized computer. The components of a distributed system communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages to one another in order to achieve a common goal. Three significant challenges of distributed systems are: maintaining concurrency of components, overcoming the lack of a global clock, and managing the independent failure of components. When a component of one system fails, the entire system does not fail. Examples of distributed systems vary from SOA-based systems to microservices to massively multiplayer online games to peer-to-peer applications. Distributed systems cost significantly more than monolithic architectures, primarily due to increased needs for additional hardware, servers, gateways, firewalls, new subnets, proxies, and so on. There are many different types of implementations for the message passing mechanism, including pure HTTP, RPC connectors and message queues.
System Design
System design is the process of defining the architecture, components, interfaces and data of a software system to meet specific requirements. System design is a crucial process that ensures a system is well-structured, efficient, and can meet the evolving needs of its users. Key Concepts in Distributed System Design: Nodes or components: These are the individual computers or processes that make up the distributed system. Network: A network connects these nodes, enabling communication and data exchange. Communication: Many protocols and mechanisms (REST, RPC, MQ) are used for communication between nodes. Transparency: Users should not be aware of the underlying complexity of the distributed nature of the system. Scalability: The system should be able to handle increasing load and data volume by adding more nodes. Availability: The system should remain operational and accessible even if some nodes fail. Consistency: Ensuring data consistency across multiple nodes is a significant challenge. Fault Tolerance: The system should be designed to handle failures gracefully and continue functioning. Security: How secure should the system be? Consider factors like authentication, encryption, and access controls. Durability: How durable should the system be? What is the timeframe for data retention.
Application Programming Interface
API is a set of rules and protocols that allows different software applications to communicate with each other. API authentication ensures that right person is accessing the right data. A web service helps us expose the functionality or data of an application to another applications over world wide web. Web services provide a web-based interface that makes the services of an application available to other applications on the web.
Scalability
Scalability is the ability of a system to handle an increased workload without loosing performance. The ability of a system to handle a growing amount of work by adding resources. The ability of a system to handle increasing traffic, data, or users without breaking or slowing down.
Domain Name System
DNS is a hierarchical and distributed name service that provides a naming system for computers, services and other resources on the internet or other Internet Protocol (IP) networks. GeoDNS (Geographical Domain Name System) is a DNS service that redirects users to different servers based on their geographical location, optimizing traffic flow for better performance and availability. It works by identifying a user's IP address, determining their geographic location using IP geolocation databases, and then returning the IP address of a server that is closest or most appropriate for that region.
Load-balancer
Load balancing is the method of distributing network traffic equally across a pool of resources that support an application. Modern applications must process millions of users simultaneously and return the correct text, videos, images, and other data to each user in a fast and reliable manner. To handle such high volumes of traffic, most applications have many resource servers with duplicate data between them. A load balancer is a device that sits between the user and the server group and acts as an invisible facilitator, ensuring that all resource servers are used equally. Companies usually have their application running on multiple servers. Such a server arrangement is called a server farm. User requests to the application first go to the load balancer. The load balancer then routes each request to a single server in the server farm best suited to handle the request. Benefits of load balancer: load balancer improves an application’s availability, scalability, security and performance. We can use load balancing to make these tasks easier: Run application server maintenance or upgrades without application downtime. Load-balancer allows us to roll out software updates without taking the whole service down.
Rate-limiter
Rate limiting is the technique for limiting the number of request that can be made to a web service in a given period of time. For monetising an API rate limit is very important when you want to charge your client based on number of request made.
API Gateway
API gateway acts as a single entry point for handling client requests to various backend services. It acts as a reverse proxy, routing requests to the appropriate services and can also handle cross-cutting concerns like authentication, authorization, rate limiting, caching, logging, monitoring, protocol translation and load balancing. API gateway accepts API requests from a client, processes them based on defined policies, directs them to the appropriate services, and combines the responses for a simplified user experience.
Caching
Caching is a techniques of storing frequently accessed data in a temporary storage location to improve the system performance. Caching helps to reduce the time and resources required to fetch the data from the original source by serving it quickly from the cache. Caching is a technique that allows frequently accessed data to be stored in memory so that it can be quickly retrieved without having to go back to the database every time. Web browser caches web pages to load them faster on subsequent visits. Database server caches frequently accessed query results to avoid expensive database queries. Cacheable data: data that does not changes at all are the best candidate for caching and data that does not frequently changes can also be cached for certain period to time. Cache reduces latency and load on the database.
Cache-eviction Policy
Cache eviction policy is an algorithm used to decide which data to remove from a cache when it is full, in order to make space for new data. The goal is to keep the most relevant and frequently accessed data in the cache to maintain performance. Why cache eviction is important: Manages limited cache size: Caches have a finite amount of memory, and eviction ensures old or unused data is removed to make space for new data. Improves performance: By keeping the most relevant data in the cache, eviction policies can increase the cache hit rate and reduce access times. Ensures data consistency: Eviction can help ensure that data in the cache is not stale, especially for data that changes frequently.
LRULFUFIFO
Removes the data that has not been accessed for the longest time. When a new item is added and the cache is full, the oldest item is evicted. Removes the data that has been accessed the fewest number of times. This policy assumes that infrequently used items are less valuable and less likely to be needed soon. Removes the oldest data that was added to the cache, regardless of how frequently it was accessed.
Round-robinTTL
Chooses a random item to evict when the cache is full. This is very fast but unpredictable and not suitable for most applications. Evicts items after a specific amount of time has passed since they were added or last accessed, making it useful for time-sensitive data.
Batch Processing
Batch processing is a method for automating and processing a high volume of data or tasks in groups, known as batches, at scheduled intervals without user interaction. Batch processing helps minimize human intervention or manual processes that have been required to complete certain tasks in the past, including processing reports at the end of each day, week, or month. Batch processing is the running of a software job in an automated and unattended way. A user schedules a job to run and then waits for a processing system to run it. A job is scheduled to run at a configured time of day or when an event occurs or when computer resources are available. When to use batch processing: When we want to transfer a huge number of data from the source to the destination.
SAGA
Saga pattern is a design pattern used in microservices architecture to manage distributed transactions. This allows for a sequence of local transactions across multiple services, ensuring data consistency even when individual services might fail. It achieves this by defining a series of compensating transactions that can be executed to undo previous operations if a failure occurs. There are two main ways to implement: choreography and orchestration. Orchestration: a central coordinator service manages the saga, instructing each service when to perform its local or compensating transaction. Choreography: services react to events published by other services, deciding whether to perform their local transaction or a compensating transaction based on the event. Compensating transactions: for each local transaction, a corresponding compensating transaction is defined. When a transaction fails, the saga executes these compensating transactions in reverse order to undo the changes made by the successful transactions. Saga pattern relies on asynchronous communication to trigger subsequent transactions and compensating actions ensuring loose coupling between services. In monolith app when we have a set of operations which we want to perform as a unit, we can simply use database transaction but in case of microservices based application when we have
Coockies and Session
Cookie data is stored on the client’s computer. Cookies can be set to expire after a specific time period or when the browser is closed. In some cases, it is essential that all requests from a client are sent to the same server for the duration of a session, for example when a client is putting items in a shopping cart and then completing the purchase. Maintaining the connection between client and server is known as session persistence. Without session persistence, data has to be synchronized across servers and potentially fetched multiple times which results in performance loss. Session data is stored on the server.

title: SAM
content:
About
I have been worked as a Java developer for about three and half years. During that period, I developed software that helps power companies manage the flow of electricity between producers and consumers of electricity — kind of like how banks manage the flow of money between accounts. My technical stack includes Java, Python, Spring Boot, Hibernate JPA, Kafka, SQL, NoSQL databases. Besides that I have sound knowledge of Object-oriented design principles, Design Patterns, Data Structures, Algorithms and System design. Microservices architecture and Patterns. I have worked on the design and developement of a new project namely SAMAST from the start to finish. Also worked on the enhancement and debugging of the existing projects. My main roles:
  • Developing REST APIs
  • Performance optimization
  • Debugging
  • Code review
  • Writing scheduled job
  • Communicating with UI team
I am mainly interested in writing clean and optimal code, performance optimization, debugging and distributed system.
  • Used flyweight pattern with BigDecimal in DSM calculation.
  • Used strategy to calculate DSM amount based on selected CERC method.
  • Used multithreading for fetching data from both MySQL and MongoDB for DSM calculation.
  • Used subquery in fetch data one go. Data access code inside for loop.
  • Used caching for unchanging data.
  • Solved N + 1 problem.
Power Domain
Power domain deals with Scheduling, Forecasting, Accounting, Metering and Settlement of transaction in electricity. Power system consists of a set of generators who generates power, a set of consumers of comsumes power, a set of distributers and a single operator know as power grid operator. Scheduling is the process of planning the generation and flow of electricity to meet consumer demand in real-time and submitting day-ahead or intra-day schedules of power generation and consumption to the system operator. Forecasting predicts how much power people or companies will need tomorrow or next week. Open access allows a consumer to buy electricity from a source of their choice. Power domain deals with Scheduling, Forecasting, Accounting, Metering and Settlement of transaction in electricity. Power system consists of a set of generators who generates power, a set of consumers of comsumes power, a set of distributers and a single operator know as power grid operator. Scheduling is the process of planning the generation and flow of electricity to meet consumer demand in real-time and submitting day-ahead or intra-day schedules of power generation and consumption to the system operator. Forecasting predicts how much power people or companies will need tomorrow or next week. Open access allows a consumer to buy electricity from a source of their choice. In traditional way it is grid operator that decides which consumers will consume power from which generators.
SAMAST
SAMAST is an acronym for Scheduling, Accounting, Metering and Settlement of Transactions in Electricity. Main goal of the SAMAST project was to automate all these tasks like Scheduling, Accounting and Settlement which previousely used to be done manually by human using email and excepjhghgf SAMAST modules consisted of 12 modules, I have worked on 6 modules namely Scheduling, Forecasting, Accounting, Open-access, Settlement.

title: Noted
content: Stateful architecture remembers client data from one request to the next, while a stateless architecture does not. CQRS pattern help us scale read and write operations independently. Search tree have the initial state at the root. Branches of search tree are actions. Search tree nodes corresponds to states in the state space of the problem. Search tree have repeated states: such repeated states may make the search tree infinite. Latency measured in milliseconds (ms) represents the time taken for a single query to execute. Latency is the time it takes to process one request; throughput is how many requests are handled per second. Throughput measured in operations per second or transactions per second represents the volume of data processed per unit time. Latency focuses on the speed of each operations while throughput focuses on the volume of operations that can be handled per unit time. Data structure is a way of organizing data so that it can be used (updated, queried and deleted) effectively. Heartbeat is a small, regular signal sent between components to check if they’re still alive and responsive. SPOF is a component in our system whose failure can bring down the entire system down. Authentication verifies the identity of a user or service and authorization determines their access rights. Authentication is the process of verifying a user's identity while authorization is the process of determining what actions a user can perform. HTTP keep-alive or persistent connection feature allows a single TCP connection to be reused for multiple HTTP requests and responses. A rate limiter controls how many requests a client can make within a specific timeframe. Library is a tool that provides a specific functionality. Framework is a set of tools and guidelines for building the application. Transaction: a series of database operations that either all succeed or they all fail. Normalization is technique of organizing the data into multiple related tables to minimize data redundancy. Primary key: candidate key chosen to uniquely identify each row of data in a table. Foreign key: an attribute in table which is used to define its relationship with another table. DAO is a design pattern to implement persistent layer of the Java application and reduces coupling between business logic and persistent logic. Pluggable code is a system architecture where components can be easily added, removed or replaced without changing existing code. Singleton: client-server model where client class can have many objects but server class must have a single object. Rust: at any given time, you can have either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references. Rust: to use a value without taking ownership, you use references, a concept known as borrowing. References must always be valid, they cannot outlive the data they refer to.

title: Think
content:
Consider writing our entire application using log4j API. One day we decide to switch back to logback then we will have to change all the classes that uses log4j API.
=======================================================================================================================
The server programs such as database and web servers can simultaneously serves multiple clients. An approach for building a server application would be to create a new thread each time a request arrives and service this new request in the newly created thread. While this approach seems simple to implement, it has significant disadvantages. A server that creates a new thread for every request would spend more time and consume more system resources in creating and destroying threads than processing actual requests.
=======================================================================================================================
Resources: Memory, files, network connections

title: CMP
content:
Synchronous vs Asychronous Commnunication
Communication between two services (A, B) is synchronous or blocking when service A call service B and service A will keep waiting till the service B returns the response. An event is any type of action, incident, or change that's identified or recorded by software or applications. Topic is basic unit for categorizing data in kafka. Kafka stream API can be used to transform or aggregate topics before reaching to consumers. Message queue allows asynchoronous communication between microservices. Message queues enables asynchronous communication between different parts of a system.

title: Java
content:
Java
Java is a high-level, general purpose, object-oriented, generic, concurrent and functional programming language. Java enables programmer to write once, run anywhere (WORA), meaning compiled java code can run on all platform without the need to recompile. Java applications are typically compiled to bytecode that can run on any JVM regardless of the underlying computer architecture. Java runtime provides dynamic capabilities such as reflection and runtime code modification that are typically not available in traditional compiled languages. Object-oriented: Java is a complete object-oriented programming language. Multithreaded: Java has support for multithreading at the language level. Dynamic: Java support dynamic code modification through reflection. Simple: Java is simple and easy to learn. Portable: Java program is compiled to bytecode insted of machine code. Secure: Because execution of bytecode is under the control of the JVM. Compiled and interpreted: Java program is compiled then interpreted by the JVM. High performance:Java bytecode executes with high performance because of JIT. Platform independent: Java is independent of operating system platform. Architecture neutral: Java is independent of hardware platform.
Java Developement Kit
Java development kit is collection of tools which is used to compile, debug and run a program developed using the java technology. JDK is the superset of JRE which is superset of JVM. JDK includes a JRE, a compiler, a debugger, an archiver and other tools that are used in Java development. javac: Compiler that read Java source code and translates them into bytecode which is executed by the JVM. javadoc: Documentation generator that read Java source code and generates documentation. javap: Disassembler that is used to disassemble Java class file. jdb: Debugger command-line debugging tool used to find and fix bugs in Java programs. jar: Archiver general purpose Java archiver.
Java Virtual Machine
Java virtual machine is an abstract computing machine which loads, verifies and executes bytecode. Bytecode is the instruction set of the java virtual machine. Java virtual machine is divided into three main subsystem:
  • Class loader subsystem
  • Runtime data area
