  • Execution engine
Verify: Bytecode verifier will verify whether the generated bytecode is proper or will get the verification error.
Prepare: All static variables memory will be allocated and assigned with default values.
Resolve: All symbolic memory references are replaced with the original references from method area.
Class-loader
Class loader subsystem is responsible for loading, linking, verifying and initializing java class file. Runtime data areas contain class area, heap area, stack areas and PC registers. Execution engine contains an bytecode interpreter, JIT compiler and memory manager. The class loader is part of the java virtual machine which is used to load class files during execution of a program. Application classes and all the dependencies from imported JARs are loaded by the system class loader. Bootstrap class loader loads JDK core classes (rt.jar). Extension class loader loads classes defined in lib\ext directory. System class loader loads classes from the classpath. Custom class loader loads classes from third party libraries.
Java Memory
Class Area: Class elements like constant pool, class fields, constructor codes, method codes are stored here. All executing threads share this part of the JVM memory area. Heap: Heap is the area of memory where states of the each objects are stored. Heap memory is not thread-safe as it is shared by all threads of a program. Heap is dynamic area of memory. Stack: Stack memory stores information about the active methods of a java program which grows and shrinks as new methods are added and removed to stack memory. Stack memory gets automatically allocated and deallocated. Access to stack memory is fast as compared to heap memory. Stack memory is thread-safe as each thread has its own stack memory. Native Stack: Stack memory stores information about the active native methods. PC Registers: PC registers stores the address of currently executing instruction.
Constant Pool
Constant pool is a part of .class file that contains constants needed to run the code of that class. These constants include literals specified by the programmer and symbolic references generated by compiler. Symbolic references are basically names of fields, methods and classes referenced from the code. These references are used by the linker to link our code. System.out.println("Hello, world!"); 0: getstatic #2 3: ldc #3 5: invokevirtual #5 #2 Field java/lang/System.out:Ljava/io/PrintStream; #3 String Hello, world! #5 Method java/io/PrintStream.println:(Ljava/lang/String;)V
Local Variable
Variable declared inside method is called local variable. Local variables are stored on stack. Local variables must be initialized before first use. Local variables can only be accessed by the code inside the method. Memory for local variable is allocated when corresponding method is called. Local vars exist only while the method in which they are declared is executing.
Instance Variable
Instance variables are declared inside class and outside method. Instance variables are initialized to their default values. Instance variables are stored in the heap area of memory. Instance variables represents state of the object. Instance variables are accessed using object of the class. Instance variables can not be accessed directly from static context. Instance variables are created when an object of the class is created. Instance variables are destroyed when the object is destroyed. Each objects of a class have separate copy of the instance variables.
Static Variable
Static variables are created inside class and outside method with static keyword. Static variables are stored in the class area of the java virtual machine. Static variables are initialized only once when the class is loaded. Static variables belongs to the class and not to object. Static variables can be accessed directly by the class name. Lifetime of an static variable is the same as the lifetime of the program. Static variables can be accessed by both instance method and static method. Memory allocation only happens once when the class is loaded in the memory.
Static Block
The static block is a block of statement inside a java class that will be executed when a class is first loaded into the JVM. A static block helps to initialize the static data members just like constructors helps us to initialize instance members. A static block executes once in the life cycle of any program, another property of static block is that it executes before the main method. Instance initialization blocks are used to initialize the instance data members. Instance initialization blocks runs each time a new instance is created. Instance initialization blocks comes in the order in which they appear. Order of execution:
  • Instance initialization block of the superclass.
  • Constructors of the superclass.
  • Instance initialization blocks of the class.
  • Constructors of the class.
Method
Methods are used to perform a simple task of the program. Method header provides interface to the other methods. Methods form the object's interface with the outside world. Objects define their interaction with the outside world through the methods that they expose. Method has two parts one is header (interface) and other is body (implementation). Instance method is a method that belongs to instances of a class. Instance method defines behaviour of the objects. Instance method can be overriden, abstract and concrete. Instance method can refer to this and super keyword. Instance method can not be accessed from the static context. Instance method can access instance variables, static variables, instance methods and static methods. Static method is a method which belongs to the class and not to the object. Static method can not access instance variables or methods of the class. Static method can only access static variables or methods of the class. Static method is associated with the class in which it is defined. Static method cannot refer to this or super keyword. Static method is statically bound to method call. Static method can be accessed directly by the class name without any object.
Autoboxing and Unboxing
Automatic conversion of primitive types to corresponding wrapper class is known as autoboxing. Automatic conversion of wrapper class to corresponding primitive types is known as unboxing. Autoboxing and unboxing is performed at compile time by compiler. Java compiler applies autoboxing when a primitive value:
  • Assigned to a variable of the corresponding wrapper class.
  • Passed as param to a method that expects an object of the corresponding wrapper class.
Java compiler applies unboxing when a wrapper class object:
  • Assigned to a variable of the corresponding primitive type.
  • Passed as param to a method that expects an argument of corresponding primitive type.
Access Modifiers
Access modifiers are used for defining accessibility of classes, methods and fields. default: Declarations are visible only within the package. private: Declarations are visible within the class only. protected: Declarations are visible within the package or all subclasses. public: Declarations are visible everywhere. Class modifiers:
  • final: Class cannot be inherited by other classes.
  • abstract: Class cannot be used to create objects.
Method modifiers:
  • final: Method cannot be overridden or modified.
  • static: Method belongs to the class rather than an object.
  • abstract: Method does not have a body which is provided by the subclass.
  • synchronized: Method can only be accessed by one thread at a time.
Field modifiers:
  • final: Field cannot be modified.
  • static: Field belongs to the class rather than an object.
  • transient: Fields are skipped when serializing the object containing them.
  • volatile: Field value is not cached thread-locally and is always read from the memory.
Constructor
Constructor is a kind of method that is used to initialize newly created objects. Constructor has the same name as the class name in which it is declared. Constructor is automatically called upon creation of a new object. Constructor cannot be abstract, static, final and synchronized. Constructor can be overloaded. Constructor can not have return type. Constructor can be parameterized or can have zero parameter. Constructor with zero parameter is known as default constructor. Constructor overloading is the special kind of method overloading. Parent class constructor is called before the subclass constructor is called. The process of calling a constructor from another constructor is called constructor chaining. Expose multiple ways of constructing an object using constructors chaining. Constructor chaining makes our code more readable and avoid duplicate code. Constructor chaining can be done in two ways:
  • By using this keyword for chaining constructors in the same class.
  • By using super keyword for chaining constructors from the parent class.
Immutable Class
An immutable class is a class whose instances cannot be modified after they are created. Once an object of an immutable class is initialized, its state remains constant throughout its lifetime. Immutable class is good for multithreaded application as state of the object cannot be changed.
  • Make all fields final and private.
  • Do not provide setter mathods.
  • Declare class as final to prevent subclasses to override methods.
  • The constructor is responsible for setting the initial state of the object.
  • When the instance fields include references to mutable objects:
  • Performs a deep copy of these mutable objects to prevent external references from modifying the internal state.
  • Getter methods for these mutable objects also return deep copies or unmodifiable views rather than direct references to the internal mutable objects. This prevents external code from obtaining a reference and altering the internal state.
Exception
Exception is an event that disrupts the normal flow of a program during its execution. When an exception occurs, the program's normal execution path is interrupted and the Java virtual machine (JVM) attempts to find an appropriate exception handler to manage the error. If no handler is found, the program terminates. Exception can be divided into two board categories: faults introduced by the programmer, such as division by zero or calling a method with null reference and things out of the programmer's control, such as a user entering a garbage on the keyboard when the program expects a positive integer. Try-catch and finally blocks helps in writing the application code which may throw exceptions at runtime and gives us a chance to either recover from exception by executing alternate application logic or handle the exception to report back to the user. Which helps in preventing the ugly application crashes. Try-catch promotes separation of concern principles by avoiding mixing of business logic code and error handling code.
  • Code to be monitored for exceptions is written inside try block.
  • Catch block contains exception handling code.
  • We can use multiple catch with a single try.
  • Catch block must be written next to the try block.
  • Finally statement defines a code block to run regardless of the result.
Facts about Exception
Checked exceptions are those checked by the java compiler at compile time. Checked exceptions are forced to be caught or declared by the compiler. Checked exceptions are also called compile time exceptions. Checked exceptions can be handled. Checked exceptions can be propagated through throws. Code will not compile if checked exceptions are not handled. Checked exception represent a potential problem that the user knows can happen. Checked exception forces the user to establish an error handling policy at compile time. Unchecked exceptions are not checked by the compiler at compile time. Unchecked exceptions are also called RuntimeExceptions or Error. Unchecked exceptions does not need to be caught or declared. Unchecked exceptions are caught by the JVM. Unchecked exceptions can not be handled. Runtime exception indicates an issue with the code that can be prevented. Unchecked exceptions represent an unexpected defect in the application that happens at runtime. Unchecked exceptions does not force the user to establish an error handling policy on compile.
Lambda
Lambda is a concise way to represent anonymous functions which is primarily used to implement functional interfaces. Lambda expressions are similar to methods but without name and they can be defined inside the body of a method. Lambda enable functional programming paradigms in Java, allowing behavior (functions) to be passed as arguments to methods or assigned to variables. The lambda expression should have the same number of parameters and the same return type as that method. Lambda expression help us to avoid boilerplate code. Supplier randomValue = () -> Math.random(); System.out.println(randomValue.get()); Predicate<Integer> lessThan = a -> (a < 100); System.out.println(lessThan.test(101)); Consumer<String> consumer = a -> System.out.println(a); list.stream().forEach(consumer); Function<String, String> function = a -> a.toUpperCase(); System.out.println(function.apply("hello"));
Serialization
Serialization is the process of converting an object into stream of bytes. Once we have a stream of bytes we can store that into database, memory or into a file. Serialization is done to persist data or state of an object beyond the life cycle of an application. Second reason to serialize an object is to transmit data or state of an object over a network. All subtypes of a serializable class are also serializable. Serializable interface has no methods or fields and serves only to identify the semantic of being serializable. Serializability of a class is enabled by the class implementing the io.Serializable interface. Classes that do not implement this interface will not have any of their state serialized or deserialized. User user = new User("Aryan", "aryan@gmail.com", 10) FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream("usr.d") ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(fos) oos.writeObject(user) oos.close() fos.close()
Deserialization
Deserialization is the process of reconstructing an object that has been serialized before. Deserialization is the reverse process of serialization where byte stream is used to create actual java object into memory. Transient keyword is used to avoid serialization of particular field of an serializable object. Fields marked with transient will not be serialized during serialization. Deserialization process would use the default values for such fields when creating an object out of the serialized form bacause transient fields are not present in the serialized form of an object. FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("usr.d") ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(fis) User user = (User) ois.readObject() ois.close() fis.close()

title: OPT
content:
• Cache results of the queries for fast read operation which reduces load on the database.
  • Use database indexing to improve read operations.
  • Use flyweight design pattern to cache shared object states.
  • Use eager loading in hibernate instead of lazy loading.
  • When threads becomes non-blocking, your task is waiting but threads should take on next one.
  • Distributed transaction span across the multiple microservices.
  • Use interfaces for behavior and abstract classes for shared logic.
  • Prefer composition over inheritance where possible.
  • Always discuss scalability, concurrency, and thread safety.
  • Structure your solution into clear layers like controller, service, repository, and model.
  • Apply SOLID and DRY principles naturally in your reasoning.
  • Handle failures and exceptions gracefully.
  • Use join fetch or eager loading in hibernate.
  • Create indexes on highly selected columns.
